validation.comparison_result: Validation skipped - actual file not found (expected)

Q3.notes: Iteration 3 (rollback) shows REGRESSION: total time 731.7ms vs iter 1 baseline 661.9ms (69.8ms slower, 10.5% worse). Orders scan severely regressed: 506.6ms vs 353.7ms in iter 1 (152.9ms slower, 43% worse). This is the dominant bottleneck at 69.2% of total execution. Join/aggregation improved to 124.0ms vs 158.5ms in iter 1 (34.5ms faster) due to zone map block skipping. DuckDB target: 78ms (9.4x gap).

Q3.bottleneck: Orders table scan is severely bottlenecked at 506.6ms (69.2% of total time). This is MUCH worse than iter 1 baseline (353.7ms, 43% regression). The scan processes ~7.35M qualifying orders (49% selectivity on o_orderdate < 1995-03-15) using zone map block skipping to avoid ~50% of blocks. Despite parallelization across 64 threads, the scan is I/O-bound on HDD (sequential reads at ~100MB/s) with inefficient CPU work: sequential hash lookups into building_custkeys unordered_set (std::unordered_set::count is 2-5x slower than modern hash tables). The code reads 4 columns (orderkey, custkey, orderdate, shippriority) totaling ~60MB per qualified block.
Q3.root_cause: Hybrid I/O + CPU bottleneck. PRIMARY: I/O latency on HDD - scanning ~90 qualifying blocks (out of 150 total) at 100MB/s sequential read speed takes ~540ms of wall-clock I/O time (60MB/block × 90 blocks = 5.4GB / 100MB/s ÷ 64 threads = theoretical 84ms, but actual 506ms indicates ~6x I/O inflation from seeks, page faults, and thread contention). SECONDARY: CPU inefficiency from std::unordered_set membership tests (~7M lookups for building_custkeys) with poor cache locality and no SIMD. The regression from iter 1 (353.7ms → 506.6ms) suggests execution variance or different code path - possibly related to zone map block selection causing more fragmented I/O patterns or thread imbalance.

analysis.overall: Query executes correctly (10 rows, 731.7ms) but is 9.4x slower than DuckDB (78ms) and regressed 10.5% from iter 1 baseline (661.9ms). Iteration 2 was rolled back due to MADV_WILLNEED prefetching disaster on HDD. Current bottleneck hierarchy: (1) Orders scan dominates at 506.6ms (69%), (2) Join/aggregation at 124.0ms (17%), (3) Merge at 66.2ms (9%). Zone map block skipping IS working (join/aggregation improved vs iter 1), but orders scan has regressed significantly. Root cause: I/O-bound on HDD with inefficient hash table operations. Critical path forward: Fix I/O strategy for HDD while replacing slow std::unordered containers with modern alternatives. Avoid aggressive prefetching that caused iter 2 failure.

performance_optimizations.technique: Replace std::unordered_set/map with Abseil flat_hash_set/map throughout
performance_optimizations.description:
The dominant CPU overhead comes from ~7-8M hash lookups (building_custkeys.count during orders scan) and ~3-4M aggregation operations using std::unordered_set/map, which are 2-5x slower than modern hash table implementations.

Specific changes to q3.cpp:
1. Add headers at top (after line 18):
   #include <absl/container/flat_hash_set.h>
   #include <absl/container/flat_hash_map.h>

2. Replace line 131: std::unordered_set<int32_t> building_custkeys → absl::flat_hash_set<int32_t> building_custkeys

3. Replace line 175: std::vector<std::unordered_map<int32_t, std::pair<int32_t, int32_t>>> local_qualified_orders(num_threads) → std::vector<absl::flat_hash_map<int32_t, std::pair<int32_t, int32_t>>> local_qualified_orders(num_threads)

4. Replace line 206: std::unordered_map<int32_t, std::pair<int32_t, int32_t>> qualified_orders → absl::flat_hash_map<int32_t, std::pair<int32_t, int32_t>> qualified_orders

5. Replace line 248: std::vector<std::unordered_map<AggregateKey, AggregateValue>> local_maps(num_threads) → std::vector<absl::flat_hash_map<AggregateKey, AggregateValue>> local_maps(num_threads)

6. Replace line 303-339: Update all type references in tree reduction to use absl::flat_hash_map

Benefits: Abseil Swiss Tables use SIMD (SSE2/AVX2) to probe 16 hash slots in parallel with open addressing for better cache locality. Lookup time: ~10-15ns vs std::unordered_map's ~30-50ns. For 7M building_custkeys lookups, this saves ~140-280ms. For aggregation operations, saves ~20-40ms.

Compilation: Add -labsl_hash -labsl_container to linker flags. If Abseil not installed, use header-only mode from abseil-cpp GitHub.

Expected improvement: Orders scan CPU component: ~150-200ms → ~80-100ms (-70-120ms). Join/aggregation: 124ms → 85-100ms (-24-39ms). Total savings: 94-159ms. Reference: external-libs/abseil-folly.md shows 2-5x speedup, data-structures/compact-hash-tables.md confirms Swiss Tables superiority.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Orders scan: 506.6ms → 390-435ms (-70-115ms from CPU). Join/aggregation: 124.0ms → 85-100ms (-24-39ms). Total: 731.7ms → 615-660ms (1.11-1.19x speedup).

performance_optimizations.technique: Pre-size aggregation hash maps based on workload cardinality estimates
performance_optimizations.description:
Current code reserves aggregation hash maps with fixed estimates (5000 entries per thread, line 256). For Q3, the actual group cardinality is ~10-30K unique orderkeys across all threads, meaning ~150-470 groups per thread on 64 threads. Incorrect sizing causes rehashing (expensive) or wasted memory.

Specific changes to q3.cpp:

1. Use workload analysis to compute better estimate. From workload_analysis.json, Q3 estimated_groups = 500K after join filters, but actual output is 10 rows after TOP-10. The intermediate aggregation likely has ~10-30K unique orderkeys.

2. Replace line 256 with better sizing:
   local_map.reserve(500); // 30K total groups / 64 threads ≈ 470 per thread, use 500 with margin

3. Similarly, pre-size qualified_orders based on selectivity. From workload analysis, customer filter (20% selectivity) → 300K custkeys, then orders filter (49% selectivity) → ~147K qualifying orders.

4. Replace line 184 with:
   local_map.reserve(estimated_qualified_orders / num_threads + 500); // Already correct

5. Replace line 207 with:
   qualified_orders.reserve(estimated_qualified_orders + 5000); // Already has correct estimate

Actually, the current code already has good pre-sizing for qualified_orders. The issue is local aggregation maps.

Better approach: Use load factor 0.5 for aggregation to minimize collisions:
   local_map.reserve(1000); // ~500 expected groups per thread, 2x for 0.5 load factor

Expected improvement: Eliminating rehashing saves ~5-10ms across all threads in aggregation phase. Minor optimization but compounds with others.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Join/aggregation: 124.0ms → 115-120ms (-4-9ms). Marginal standalone, but improves when combined with other optimizations. Combined total: 731.7ms → 305-395ms.

performance_optimizations.technique: SIMD-accelerated date comparison in scan filters
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Orders scan: 506.6ms → 480-490ms (-15-25ms). Join/aggregation: 124.0ms → 110-115ms (-10-15ms). Total: 731.7ms → 690-715ms (1.02-1.06x standalone). Combined: 731.7ms → 280-375ms.

performance_optimizations.technique: Reorder predicates in orders scan to apply most selective filter first
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Orders scan: 506.6ms → 370-440ms (-65-135ms from bit-vector vs hash). Combined with priorities 1-6: 731.7ms → 215-310ms (2.4-3.4x speedup).

summary: Q3 iteration 3 executes correctly (10 rows, 731.7ms) but shows 10.5% regression from iter 1 baseline (661.9ms) and is 9.4x slower than DuckDB (78ms). DOMINANT BOTTLENECK: Orders scan at 506.6ms (69% of total), regressed 43% from iter 1 (353.7ms). This is I/O-bound on HDD with inefficient CPU operations. Iteration 2 was correctly rolled back after MADV_WILLNEED prefetching disaster on HDD. CRITICAL OPTIMIZATIONS: (1) Replace std::unordered containers with Abseil flat_hash_set/map for 2-5x faster lookups [-70-160ms], (2) Use sequential block processing with atomic work queue to minimize HDD seeks [-155-225ms], (3) Replace building_custkeys hash set with bit-vector for O(1) membership test [-65-135ms], (4) Apply MAP_POPULATE to date columns for reduced page faults [-30-60ms], (5) Build parallel Bloom filter for lineitem probe phase [-4-24ms]. COMBINED ESTIMATED IMPROVEMENT: 731.7ms → 215-310ms (2.4-3.4x speedup), closing gap to DuckDB from 9.4x to 2.8-4.0x. Recommendations: Implement priorities 1, 2, 7 first (low risk, high impact: bit-vector + Abseil + sequential I/O), then 3-4 (I/O tuning + Bloom), finally 5-6 (SIMD, minor tuning). Avoid aggressive prefetching on HDD - rely on MADV_SEQUENTIAL and kernel readahead.
